title: First 90 Days at FAANG — A Guide for SAP Engineers
description: How to successfully navigate the first 90 days at FAANG as a Staff+ engineer coming from SAP. Covers pre-boarding strategy, week 1 survival, building your technical reputation in months 1-2, delivering your first project by month 3, and the specific mistakes SAP engineers make in FAANG culture.

description.value:
The 2-4 weeks before your start date are your asymmetric advantage window. Most FAANG new hires spend this time relaxing. You should spend it building a foundation that makes your first week boring — in a good way. The goal is to have all environment setup, team context, and pre-reading done before you have a badge.

Tech Stack Familiarization: If your team uses Go, Python, or Java — spend 10 hours writing small programs in that language. FAANG engineers are polyglots and the expectation is that you ramp on a new language within two weeks. Do not waste your first week learning syntax. If the team uses Kubernetes, Kafka, gRPC, or Bazel — watch the internal tech talks and read the official docs. You don't need to be productive on day 1, but you should understand what each technology is for.

Read the Team's Design Docs: Find the team's design doc archive. Read the last 5-6 design docs from the past 6 months. Pay attention to the decision log — why did they choose technology X over Y? What tradeoffs were made? This gives you conversational depth in your first week. You won't understand all of it, but you will ask better questions.

Set Up 1:1s Before Starting: Reach out to your manager 2 weeks before your start date. Ask for a 30-minute call. Introduce yourself, express excitement, and ask: "What is the most important thing I can do in my first week to set us up for success?" Ask your manager to introduce you via email to the team and 2-3 cross-team partners before you start. Schedule 30-minute introductory 1:1s with each of them during your first week.

SAP-Specific Pre-Boarding: The single most important preparation is removing SAP jargon from your vocabulary. From day 0, stop saying RFC, BAPI, LUW, BADI, IDoc, ALE, and SM50 in any work communication. Instead, internalize the FAANG equivalents: gRPC for RFC, plugin architecture for BADI, event-driven messaging for ALE/IDoc, thread pool monitoring for SM50. Your manager and team will form an impression of your engineering breadth within the first week — if every sentence contains SAP terminology, they will categorize you as a specialist, not a Staff+ engineer.

The most common SAP engineer mistake in week 1 at FAANG: trying to prove value immediately. SAP consulting culture rewards the person who walks in and immediately identifies problems and proposes solutions. FAANG culture rewards the person who listens, observes, and learns before acting.

The goal of week 1 is to be boring. Your manager expects you to spend week 1 doing exactly three things: (1) getting access to everything, (2) setting up your development environment, and (3) learning. That's it. Do not propose a re-architecture of the build system in week 1. Do not identify "critical problems" in the codebase. Do not write a 10-page strategy document. Every week-1 hero who tried this has gotten the same feedback: "Slow down. Focus on learning."

Week 1 technical checklist: Get your workstation set up with all security credentials, VPN, internal certificates, and code access. Clone the main repository and verify you can build and run tests. Set up your IDE with the team's recommended plugins and linters. Join all team Slack/Discord channels and introduce yourself. Read the team wiki, design doc archive, and postmortems from the last 6 months. Study the on-call rotation documentation — understand what happens when a P0 incident fires at 3 AM.

Week 1 social checklist: Attend every meeting you are invited to — even optional ones. Sit silently in design reviews and absorb how decisions are made. Write down the vocabulary you don't understand and look it up later. Learn the team's decision-making process: who has authority to approve design docs? How does the roadmap planning cycle work? What is the team's definition of done?

Week 1 deliverable: By end of week 1, write a 1-page summary of your team's top 3 technical challenges as you understand them. Share it with your manager. Do not propose solutions. The purpose is to show you are paying attention and to calibrate your understanding against your manager's perspective. Your manager will correct misalignments and you will build trust by showing you are learning, not prescribing.

sections.title: Month 1 — Building Relationships and Technical Credibility

description.value:
Month 1 is the technical listening tour. Your goal is to build relationships with every team member and cross-team partner, understand the system architecture from multiple perspectives, and start building trust through small contributions — not major architectural proposals.

Schedule 1:1s with everyone: Your manager, skip-level (your manager's manager), every engineer on your team (including junior engineers — they have context you need), the tech lead, the product manager, the TPM, your key cross-team dependencies (the team whose API you will consume), and the SRE team that handles on-call. Each 1:1 should be 30 minutes. The purpose is to understand what each person works on, what their pain points are, and what they consider the team's biggest challenges.

The technical listening tour: Ask questions. Do not propose solutions. When you see a design decision you disagree with, resist the urge to critique. Instead, ask: "Can you help me understand the tradeoffs that led to this design?" The answer will reveal either (a) a constraint you didn't know about, or (b) an actual blind spot. In case (b), do not say "I think you should change this" — say "Fascinating. I would have approached it differently but I see now why this works. Let me think about it more." Over the next week, write a short document analyzing the tradeoff and share it one-on-one with the original author. That builds trust.

Contribute a small fix: By the end of month 1, you should have shipped something — even a documentation fix, a test improvement, or a small bug. This signals that you are operational. Do not attempt a major architectural refactor in month 1. You don't have the context yet.

SAP-specific advice for month 1: When someone asks about your background, give a short, non-technical answer: "I spent the last 8 years designing distributed systems for enterprise integrations." Offer an SAP detail only if asked. If someone dismissively says "Oh, you worked on SAP?" treat it as a neutral question, not a challenge. Say: "Yes — I built large-scale data pipelines on SAP HANA. The distributed query execution engine is surprisingly sophisticated. Happy to do a brown bag on it if people are interested."

Month 2 is about identifying your first impact. The key is finding high-impact, low-risk projects. You need a win that builds momentum and visibility without requiring deep system knowledge you haven't built yet.

The low-hanging fruit strategy: Look for operational pain points that everyone complains about but no one has prioritized. A build that takes 45 minutes and could be 15 minutes with better caching. A monitoring dashboard that doesn't show the right metrics. A deployment script that requires 6 manual steps. A flaky test that fails 20% of the time. These are gold mines for month 2. They are visible, low-risk, and build goodwill across the team.

How to identify these projects: Review the on-call handoff notes from the last 3 months. Look for recurring issues. Read the team's bug tracker and look for tickets tagged "tech debt" or "improvement" that have been open for 6+ months. Ask the SRE team: "If you could fix one thing about this service that would make your on-call easier, what would it be?" That single question will generate a pipeline of high-value, low-risk projects.

SAP-specific project advice: Do not propose an SAP-related project unless the team explicitly works with SAP systems. If you join a team that builds internal tools for SAP integration, then by all means lead with your SAP expertise. But if you join a team building a generic microservice, your SAP experience is a curiosity, not a project direction. Proposing "Let's rewrite this using SAP principles" will damage your credibility. Instead, propose projects that demonstrate your systems thinking — independent of the technology stack.

value.question: You have identified two potential first projects. Which one should you choose?

branches.condition: Big Visible Project (estimated 8 weeks, high visibility with leadership, but high risk of unknown unknowns)
branches.action: Talk to 3 engineers who know the system. Validate your assumptions about scope. Write a 1-page design doc with explicit risk section. Propose a 2-week spike to reduce uncertainty before committing to full timeline.
branches.tradeoff: Higher reward if successful (visibility for promo packet), but higher risk of missing the 90-day milestone. Best choice if you have strong architectural confidence and a supportive tech lead.

branches.condition: Small Safe Project (estimated 3 weeks, lower visibility, but known path to success)
branches.action: Ship it aggressively in weeks 5-7 of your tenure. Use the confidence from this win to propose a larger project for month 4. The quick win builds social capital and proves you can execute end-to-end.
branches.tradeoff: Lower risk and guaranteed success, but may set the perception that you are a maintenance engineer rather than a strategic thinker. Build the narrative: 'I fixed the build system to learn the codebase, now I am ready for the big project.'

description.value:
Month 3 is where your trajectory is set. You must deliver one complete project end-to-end by the end of month 3. It doesn't have to be a massive initiative — a well-executed medium-sized project with a clear design doc, implementation, testing, and deployment is the goal.

Writing your first design doc: FAANG culture is document-driven. Your first design doc will be reviewed with more scrutiny than any document you have written before. Follow the team's template. Include: problem statement, goals and non-goals, system context, proposed design, alternative approaches considered, tradeoff analysis, and rollout plan. Schedule a design review meeting 1 week before you plan to start coding. Iterate on feedback. The design doc is your reputation-building tool — reviewers will form an opinion of your engineering judgment based on how well you handle the feedback cycle.

Handling the 'but you only know SAP' perception: In months 1-3, you may encounter colleagues who underestimate your breadth because of your SAP background. Do not argue. Let your work speak. When someone says "You probably haven't seen distributed consensus before", respond: "You're right, I haven't worked with Raft directly. But I have designed idempotent transaction patterns in SAP that achieve the same guarantees at the application layer. I'd love to dig into the Raft implementation. Can you point me to the key design doc?" This reframes the conversation from deficit to curiosity and capability.

Building your internal brand: By the end of month 3, you should have done at least one of: (a) presented your project at the team's weekly demo, (b) written a tech note or postmortem that others reference, (c) volunteered to review a design doc from another team member, or (d) given a 10-minute brown bag on a topic from your SAP experience (framed in general engineering terms). These small acts of visibility compound into a reputation as someone who is technically capable and generous with knowledge.

sections.title: SAP Engineer Culture Shocks at FAANG

description.value:
The hardest part of the transition is not technical — it's cultural. SAP and FAANG cultures are built on fundamentally different assumptions about hierarchy, ambiguity, speed, and feedback. Here are the five culture shocks that hit SAP engineers hardest in the first 90 days.

1. No Hierarchy — You Must Earn Respect, Not Rely on Title: In SAP consulting, the architect's title commands deference. At FAANG, your title gets you in the door, but your ideas earn influence. A fresh graduate may challenge your design in a review, and the room will listen to them if their reasoning is better. This is not disrespect — it's intellectual honesty. The SAP engineer's instinct is to pull rank. The FAANG expectation is to engage on the merits. If you find yourself thinking "I have 15 years of experience, I shouldn't have to explain this" — stop. You do. Explaining your reasoning to a junior engineer is not beneath you; it is how you build influence.

2. On-Call — You Will Be Paged at 3 AM for a Service You Didn't Write: SAP engagements rarely involve 24/7 on-call. At FAANG, on-call is a core responsibility of every engineer, regardless of tenure. You will be paged during your second or third week for a service that you have never deployed, never configured, and barely understand. This is intentional. The fastest way to learn a system is to debug it under pressure. Resistance to on-call is the fastest way to lose respect. Embrace it — the week you spend on-call will teach you more about the system than a month of reading design docs.

3. Ambiguity — There Is No Functional Spec: SAP projects start with a functional specification (FS) approved by the business. FAANG starts with a vague goal: "Reduce payment latency." There is no FS. There is no blueprint. You write the design doc that serves as the spec. The ambiguity is not a bug — it is the feature. At Staff+ level, your value is in defining the problem, not receiving a defined problem to execute.

4. Speed — Decisions Are Made in Days, Not Weeks: SAP project decisions go through change advisory boards, architecture review boards, and governance committees. FAANG decisions are made in a design review meeting or a Slack thread. If you need to change a data model, you write a quick proposal, get 2-3 LGTMs, and implement. The speed is disorienting for SAP engineers who are used to 2-week approval cycles. The key insight: you don't need permission to make decisions that are reversible. Make the decision, document it, and move on.

5. Feedback — Direct and Uncomfortable: SAP consulting feedback is indirect: "Perhaps we could consider an alternative approach." FAANG feedback is direct: "Your design doc does not address the failure scenario. This is a critical gap." The directness is not personal — it is respect. FAANG assumes you are a professional who can separate feedback about your work from feedback about you as a person. If you feel attacked when someone criticizes your design, pause and remind yourself: they are helping you improve the design before it goes to production. The fastest-growing SAP engineers are the ones who learn to say "Thank you for that feedback. Let me address it."

value.challenge: "I am 3 weeks in and I feel completely incompetent. Everyone around me seems to understand the system, and I can barely build the code. Did I make a mistake coming here?"
value.badResponse: "I should just work harder. 16-hour days will close the gap." (This leads to burnout. The gap is not about hours — it is about context you haven't built yet. Working more hours doesn't accelerate context building past the first 60 days.)
value.goodResponse: "What I am feeling is the Dunning-Kruger dip. In week 1, I didn't know what I didn't know. In week 3, I know enough to realize how much I don't know. That is not regression — it is progress. The gap between 'knowing nothing' and 'knowing everything' passes through 'knowing how much I don't know.' The SAP engineers who succeed are the ones who normalize this feeling. They say: 'I am supposed to feel incompetent in month 1. If I felt competent, I wouldn't be learning anything.' I will measure myself against my own progress, not against colleagues who have 2 years of context. My goal for month 1 is not 'be productive' — it is 'ask better questions.' I am on track."
value.pattern: reframe-the-emotion — normalize the feeling of incompetence as a predictable phase of learning. FAANG veterans know this pattern well and will respect your self-awareness. The SAP-specific trap is that in consulting, you were never in a situation where you were the least knowledgeable person in the room. FAANG puts you in that room deliberately. It is not a sign that you are failing — it is a sign that you are growing.

sections.title: Measuring Success — What 'Good' Looks Like at 90 Days

description.value:
How do you know if your first 90 days were successful? Here is the rubric that Staff+ engineers use to self-evaluate, and that managers use to calibrate your trajectory.

By day 90, you should have: (1) Delivered at least one project from design to deployment — this proves you can execute in the new environment. (2) Reviewed 5+ design docs from other team members — this proves you are contributing to team quality. (3) Been on-call for at least 2 weeks and handled at least one incident — this proves you can operate the system under pressure. (4) Given at least one team presentation or written a tech note that others reference — this proves you are building an internal brand. (5) Established 1:1 relationships with every team member and at least 3 cross-team partners — this proves you have built the relationship network to operate at Staff+ scope.

By day 90, your manager should: (1) Know your technical strengths and be able to articulate them to others. (2) Trust you with a significant project without needing daily check-ins. (3) Give positive feedback about your trajectory in 1:1s. (4) Start discussing your next project with more scope and visibility. (5) Have stopped referring to you as "the new person" in team meetings.

Red flags at 90 days: (1) You haven't shipped anything — even a small fix. (2) The team still treats you as "the SAP person who is learning" rather than a peer. (3) You have no relationships outside your immediate team. (4) You are still struggling to build the codebase on your own machine. (5) Your manager gives vague feedback about trajectory without specifics. If you see these red flags, schedule a corrective conversation with your manager in week 8-9 — don't wait for the 90-day review.

SAP-specific success metric: By day 90, the team should have stopped associating you with SAP in technical conversations. When someone asks about your background, the response should be "They are an excellent systems engineer who came from SAP" rather than "They are the SAP person on the team." This transition from identity as SAP specialist to identity as systems engineer is the single most important milestone of the first 90 days.

details: Staff+ Insight: The first 90 days at FAANG are harder for SAP engineers than for engineers from other FAANGs or startups. Not because of technical ability, but because of culture shock. The single most important predictor of success is the 'listening tour' in month 1 — SAP architects are used to being the expert who proposes solutions. At FAANG, your first month is about building relationships, not proposing solutions. If you can hold back from architecting for 30 days, you will have built the trust needed to architect for the next 3 years.
